summary: Joel announces the release of Fog Creek Copilot 2.0, a remote desktop support tool that requires zero configuration and works through firewalls. The update adds Mac support, a clever direct connect feature that silently upgrades relay connections to peer-to-peer when possible, and file transfer capabilities. The price for day passes was also cut from $10 to $5, with the reasoning that lower friction will drive more users and ultimately more corporate subscriptions. Joel highlights that the product was originally built by four interns in 12 weeks and continues to be developed by two of them who joined full-time.
key_points: Copilot 2.0 adds Mac OS X support, file transfer, and a direct connect feature that transparently upgrades relay connections to peer-to-peer for better performance
The product was originally built entirely by four interns in a 12-week summer internship, demonstrating what a small team with a good spec and open source foundations can accomplish
The day pass price was cut from $10 to $5, betting that lower prices will drive higher volume and more corporate subscriptions
The direct connect feature is architecturally clever: it guarantees 100% initial connectivity through relay servers, then silently optimizes to peer-to-peer in the background
The core value proposition is eliminating all configuration friction—no firewall rules, no port forwarding, no installation required
money_quotes: Fog Creek Copilot is a remote tech support service that lets one person control another computer remotely, much like VNC or RDC, with the advantage that it requires zero configuration, works through firewalls, and installs nothing.
One of the reasons they were able to accomplish so much in one summer is that they used open source software as a starting point.
Ben, by the way, is the only person I know who writes code in C, C++, C#, and Objective C all in the same day, while writing a book about Smalltalk at night.

It sets up the initial connection through our servers, so you get connected right away with 100% reliability. But then once you're all connected, it quietly, in the background, looks for a way to make a direct connection.
We don't want anyone to have an excuse not to use Fog Creek Copilot.

To avoid paying $10, you might actually be crazy enough to try to just talk your mom into uninstalling Norton Utilities, punching the appropriate holes in the Windows firewall, and setting up appropriate port-forwarding rules on her broadband router… but for $5, why go through the trouble?
All you need to tell your mom, or your customer, is 'Go to copilot.com, type in this number, and download and run the program you find there.' And to know that it will Just Work.
We're betting that the lower price will lead to more users, which will lead to more corporate subscriptions, which will lead to higher total revenues.
key_insight: The best technical products eliminate friction at every level—from zero-configuration networking to pricing low enough that no one bothers with workarounds.
